title: How to Get a US Driver's License
summary: A step-by-step guide to getting your driver's license as an international student, including documents needed, the written test, and the road test.

content.text: Having a driver's license in the US is useful even if you don't plan to drive regularly — it serves as a primary form of ID. The process varies by state, but the general steps are similar.
Do you need a driver's license?

If you live in a city with good public transit (New York, Chicago, Boston), you may not need one right away. But in most college towns and suburban areas, a car is practically essential for groceries, errands, and getting around.
Even if you don't drive, consider getting a state ID card — it's issued at the same DMV office, requires similar documents, and works as official photo ID everywhere.
Documents you'll need

items: Passport with valid F-1 visa
I-20 with valid travel signature
I-94 arrival record (print from i94.cbp.dhs.gov)
Proof of state residency (lease, utility bill, or bank statement)
Social Security Number — OR — letter from SSA saying you're ineligible (some states accept this)
School enrollment verification letter (some states require this)

content.text: Requirements vary significantly by state. Check your state's DMV website before going. Some states (like California) accept foreign licenses for a period, while others require you to get a local license immediately.
Step-by-step process

items.title: Study for the written test
items.description: Download your state's driver's handbook (free PDF on the DMV website). Many states offer the test in Chinese — ask when you book your appointment.

items.title: Visit the DMV
items.description: Make an appointment online if possible — walk-ins can mean 2-3 hour waits. Bring ALL your documents.

items.title: Pass the written test
items.description: Usually 25-40 multiple choice questions about road rules, signs, and safety. You need about 80% to pass.

items.title: Get your learner's permit
items.description: After passing the written test, you'll receive a learner's permit. You can now practice driving with a licensed driver in the car.

items.title: Practice driving
items.description: Practice in parking lots first, then quiet streets, then highways. Many schools have student driving clubs or you can hire a driving instructor ($40-60/hour).

items.title: Schedule and pass the road test
items.description: You'll drive with an examiner for 15-20 minutes. They'll test parking, turning, lane changes, and following traffic rules. Stay calm and check your mirrors often.

items.title: Receive your license
items.description: Some states give you a temporary paper license the same day. Your permanent card arrives by mail in 1-3 weeks.

content.text: Free practice tests are available online for every state. Search '[your state] DMV practice test' and take it several times before your appointment. The real test questions are very similar.

title: Getting Around Without a Car
summary: Public transit, ride-sharing, biking, and other ways to get around campus and your city without owning a car.

content.text: Many international students don't have a car during their first semester — and that's completely normal. Here are all the ways to get around.
Campus shuttles and buses
Most universities run free shuttle buses that connect campus, student housing, shopping areas, and nearby apartment complexes. Check your school's transportation website for routes and schedules.
Download your school's shuttle app (if available) or Google Maps — both show real-time bus locations and arrival times.
Public transit
Many college towns have city bus systems, and your student ID often gets you free or discounted rides. In larger cities, subways and light rail are efficient options.

items: Check if your student fees include a transit pass — many schools bundle this
Google Maps, Apple Maps, and Transit app all show public transit routes
Bus schedules may be reduced on weekends and holidays
Most systems accept contactless payment (tap your phone or card)

content.text: Ride-sharing apps

items.label: Uber
items.description: Most widely available. Download the app, link a payment method, and request a ride. Prices vary by demand — check before confirming.

items.label: Lyft
items.description: Similar to Uber, sometimes cheaper. Worth having both apps to compare prices.

items.label: Uber/Lyft shared rides
items.description: Share with other riders going the same direction for 30-50% savings. Takes longer but much cheaper.

content.text: Many Chinese students organize group rides to airports, Costco, or shopping centers via WeChat groups. This is a great way to save money and make friends!
Biking
Biking is popular on many campuses. Options include buying a used bike ($50-150 on Facebook Marketplace or campus bulletin boards), campus bike-share programs, or electric scooter rentals.

items: Always use a U-lock — cable locks are easily cut
Register your bike with campus police (free) in case of theft
Many campuses have free bike repair stations
Wear a helmet — it's the law in some states and always a good idea

content.text: Walking
If you live on or near campus, walking is the simplest option. Most campus facilities are within a 15-minute walk. Invest in good walking shoes and a rain jacket.
Always use crosswalks and follow pedestrian signals. Jaywalking can result in fines in some cities. At night, stick to well-lit paths and use your school's safety escort service if available.

title: Buying Your First Car in the US
summary: A practical guide to buying a used car as an international student — what to look for, how to avoid scams, and understanding insurance.

content.text: If you're in an area where a car is necessary, buying a reliable used car is often the best value. New cars lose 20-30% of their value in the first year, so a 2-4 year old used car gives you the best balance of reliability and price.
Budget planning
The purchase price is just the beginning. Factor in these ongoing costs:

items: Car insurance: $100-300/month (higher for new drivers and under 25)
Gas: $100-200/month depending on driving habits
Maintenance: oil changes, tires, brakes — budget $100/month
Registration and annual inspection: $50-200/year depending on state
Parking permit: varies by school and city

content.text: Where to buy

items.label: Dealership (used)
items.description: More expensive but offers warranties and financing. Good for peace of mind. Certified Pre-Owned (CPO) cars come with manufacturer-backed warranties.

items.label: Private seller
items.description: Usually cheaper. Check Facebook Marketplace, Craigslist, and school bulletin boards. Higher risk — always inspect the car and check its history.

items.label: Graduating student sales
items.description: International students often sell their cars before leaving. Great deals but move fast — watch WeChat groups and school forums in April-May.

items.label: Online (Carvana, CarMax)
items.description: Convenient, transparent pricing, delivery to your door. CarMax offers a 30-day return policy.

content.text: Before you buy — inspection checklist

items.title: Check the vehicle history
items.description: Run the VIN number on Carfax or AutoCheck ($40, or free at many dealerships). Look for accidents, flood damage, and odometer rollbacks.

items.title: Get a pre-purchase inspection
items.description: Pay a mechanic $100-150 to inspect the car before buying. This can save you thousands. Any honest seller will agree to this.

items.title: Test drive thoroughly
items.description: Drive on highways and local roads. Test brakes, acceleration, A/C, and listen for unusual sounds. Bring a friend who knows cars if possible.

items.title: Check the title
items.description: Make sure the title is 'clean' (not salvage or rebuilt). The seller's name should match the title.

content.text: Never wire money or pay in full before seeing the car in person. Common scams include fake listings, title washing, and odometer fraud. If a deal seems too good to be true, it probably is.
Car insurance — required by law
You must have car insurance before driving. As a new driver without US driving history, rates will be higher, but there are ways to save:

items: Get quotes from at least 3-4 companies (GEICO, Progressive, State Farm, Allstate)
Ask about student discounts and good grades discounts
Higher deductible = lower monthly payment (but more out-of-pocket if you crash)
Some insurers accept your Chinese driving history for a discount
Bundling with renters insurance often saves 10-15%

content.text: Ask fellow Chinese students in your area which insurance company they use. Often they can share referral codes for discounts, and you'll benefit from their experience with claims.
